retrieved_memories.content: When assessing used electronics for sale, prioritize verifiable device condition (e.g., screen, battery, physical damage) and functional reliability over seller-reported details like iCloud status or box presence. When evaluating used electronics with intermittent power-on and missing accessories, prioritize physical and functional reliability; treat such devices as a "parts/repair gamble" unless the seller can verify clean boot and iCloud status.

To diagnose a bike engine issue, start by asking about the symptom onset (what changed right before it started acting up), whether it starts cold, idles, dies on throttle, and whether it's a 2T or 4T. Then use a symptom tree to narrow down the cause (e.g., starts then dies → pilot/fuel flow; bogs on throttle → lean/air leak/main jet). Always request the exact symptom and the bike model to avoid random part swaps. Use short, precise phrasing like "‘05 CRF250R, 4T, started after a wash, starts cold but dies on throttle." This cuts down guesswork and prevents swapping random parts. Use a symptom tree to organize diagnostic steps: 1. Engine type (2T/4T), 2. Starts/don’t start, 3. Idles, 4. Bogs/cuts out/backfires, 5. What changed before symptoms began. To diagnose efficiently, prioritize a symptom tree that lists key attributes (engine type, start behavior, idle, throttle response, changes before onset) so they can be split into immediate fixes, checks, and ignores. (Original memory from 2026-04-29T08:30:22) Use a symptom tree approach: list possible symptoms (starts or no, idles or no, bogs/cuts out/backfires/weird noise/leak) and what changed to narrow down the issue.

To avoid starting late and piling up tasks, break the diagnostic process into clear blocks with a simple daily order: prioritize 'fuel, spark, air, chain/brakes, leaks' first, then 'plug cap, air filter, kill switch, vent', and finally cosmetic/secondary items.
When given a simple, clear instruction, User is satisfied and does not request elaboration or alternative phrasing.

To diagnose a motorcycle's starting or running issues efficiently, follow a structured checklist: check the plug, air filter, fuel flow, carb jets/float, intake boot for cracks, and compression if earlier checks fail. Avoid random part removal; start with exact symptoms and the bike's make/model. For a 2007 YZ125 2T with cold start issues, rough idle, and severe bogging when the throttle is cracked, prioritize checking the carburetor's pilot jet and fuel flow, inspecting for air leaks, and verifying the fuel filter and fuel line. This specific symptom set (cold start issues, rough idle, severe bogging on throttle) points directly to carburetor issues, especially pilot jet and fuel flow.

retrieved_memories.content: User is a dirt bike mechanic focused on simplifying local cheap-item selling with a structured, rule-based approach. User has a clear, practical framework for evaluating electronics deals based on liquidity and seller behavior. User now uses concise templates for local listings and prioritizes short, simple communication. User frequently responds to sellers who keep changing details with short, casual phrases (e.g., 'I’ll check later') to end conversations and move on. User values clear, actionable rules and structured, prioritized approaches for both mechanics and local selling. User is preparing a small used electronics listing and wants a tight, easy-to-use template to quickly fill out details (Item, Model, Condition, Tested, Included, Missing, Flaws, Price, Meetup). User now explicitly checks for locked/reset/account signed-out status on phones or consoles due to a past incident with a locked tablet. User will reject deals where price depends on half-dead junk or where seller history is suspicious (new account, weird wording, no pickup history). User requires clean, detailed listing info (exact versions, account state, battery/charger, IMEI/serial, meetup spot) before considering a deal; if missing, they will pass. User now uses short, simple phrases to respond to pushy or inconsistent sellers to maintain brevity and avoid further back-and-forth. User now prefers blunt, direct one-liners to end conversations — for example, 'Just pick a spot/time or I’m done with it.' User prefers short and blunt responses to end conversations. User uses a 30-second checklist for used electronics deals that focuses on immediate red flags (lock status, power-on, blurry photos, rushing, weird meetup) and will walk away if any red flags are present; only proceeds if all items power on and are not locked.

details.explanation: He is distrustful and defiant, so heavy lecturing would likely turn him off. But his history shows he can adjust when consequences are made clear, so he may tolerate concise pushback when it is tied directly to immediate risk.

details.predicted: low tolerance for pushback; prefers blunt, brief replies

details.judge_reason: The prediction matches the idea that he only tolerates brief, direct pushback, but it understates that he can accept some useful pushback rather than simply having low tolerance.
